title: Tactics: Pins and Skewers
summary: Master the art of pinning - immobilize enemy pieces and win material.
description: A pin is when a piece attacks an enemy piece that cannot move without exposing a more valuable piece behind it. A skewer is similar but attacks the more valuable piece first, forcing it to move and exposing the piece behind. These are essential GM-level tactical weapons.
objectives: Recognize positions where pins are possible
Understand the difference between absolute pins (to the king) and relative pins
Master the skewer - a more active version of the pin
plans: Look for enemy pieces that are lined up with their king or queen
A pinned piece cannot move - use this to attack something else
Skewers are often more forcing than pins
commonMistakes: Don't pin a piece to the king if you can't follow up
Remember: you can only win a pinned piece if you can capture it safely
A piece can only be pinned if there's something behind it
completionSummary: You've learned to create and exploit pins and skewers, essential tactical patterns.

steps.title: Control the center
steps.hint: Push the king pawn two squares.

steps.title: Black develops
steps.instruction: Black develops the bishop to c5.

steps.title: Black develops
steps.instruction: Black develops the knight to f6.

steps.title: The pinning bishop
steps.instruction: Play Bg5! This pins the knight on f6 to the queen on d8!
steps.coaching: The knight cannot move because the queen is behind it. This is a relative pin.
steps.hint: Move the bishop to g5, pinning the knight!

steps.title: Black defends
steps.instruction: Black plays h6, defending the bishop.
steps.coaching: Black has options: they can move the knight, defend it, or interpose.

steps.title: Win a piece
steps.instruction: Capture on f6! The knight was pinned and couldn't move.
steps.coaching: Because the knight was pinned to the queen, it couldn't move. We win a piece!
steps.hint: Capture on f6 with the bishop.
